subject: Chemistry
chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes; Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

questions.question: The reagent required during the preparation of a Grignard reagent to prevent its decomposition by moisture is ______.
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents are prepared in absolutely dry ether because even traces of water destroy them.

questions.question: The named reaction in which an alkyl chloride or alkyl bromide is converted into an alkyl iodide using sodium iodide in dry acetone is the ______.
questions.explanation: The Finkelstein reaction involves halogen exchange using NaI in dry acetone.

questions.question: The conjugate base formed when phenol loses a proton is called the ______.
questions.explanation: Removal of H⁺ from phenol produces the resonance-stabilised phenoxide ion.

questions.question: The major product obtained when phenol is treated with concentrated nitric acid is ______.
questions.explanation: Concentrated nitric acid converts phenol into 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, commonly called picric acid.

questions.question: The named reaction in which sodium phenoxide reacts with carbon dioxide followed by acidification to form salicylic acid is the ______.
questions.explanation: The Kolbe reaction introduces a carboxyl group into sodium phenoxide to produce salicylic acid after acidification.

questions.question: The product obtained when phenol is heated with zinc dust is ______.
questions.explanation: Heating phenol with zinc dust removes the oxygen atom, producing benzene.

questions.question: The aldehyde formed in the Reimer–Tiemann reaction of phenol is ______.
questions.explanation: The Reimer–Tiemann reaction introduces a formyl group at the ortho position of phenol to form salicylaldehyde.

questions.question: The catalyst responsible for the fermentation of glucose into ethanol is the enzyme ______.
questions.explanation: Zymase, present in yeast, catalyses the fermentation of sugars to ethanol and carbon dioxide.

questions.question: The compound formed when phenol reacts with bromine water is ______.
questions.explanation: Phenol reacts readily with bromine water to give a white precipitate of 2,4,6-tribromophenol.

questions.question: The oxidation product obtained when phenol is treated with acidified sodium dichromate is ______.
questions.explanation: Phenol is oxidised by acidified sodium dichromate to give p-benzoquinone.

questions.question: ______ solvents do not strongly solvate anions, making nucleophiles more reactive and favouring SN2 reactions.
questions.explanation: Polar aprotic solvents do not strongly solvate anions, making nucleophiles more reactive and favouring SN2 reactions.

questions.question: The stereochemical outcome observed when an SN2 reaction occurs at a chiral carbon is called ______.
questions.explanation: SN2 reactions proceed through backside attack, producing inversion of configuration (Walden inversion).

questions.question: The reagent used to convert an alcohol into an alkyl chloride while producing only gaseous by-products is ______.
questions.explanation: Thionyl chloride converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides, producing SO₂ and HCl gases that escape from the reaction mixture.

questions.question: The major product formed when phenol is nitrated with dilute nitric acid at 298 K along with p-nitrophenol is ______.
questions.explanation: Dilute nitric acid nitrates phenol mainly at the ortho and para positions to give o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol.

questions.question: The enzyme that hydrolyses sucrose into glucose and fructose before fermentation is ______.
questions.explanation: Invertase converts sucrose into glucose and fructose, which are subsequently fermented by zymase.

questions.question: The intermolecular hydrogen bonding present in ______ is responsible for its lower steam volatility than its ortho isomer.
questions.explanation: p-Nitrophenol forms intermolecular hydrogen bonds, increasing association and reducing steam volatility.

questions.question: The reaction of phenol with chloroform and aqueous sodium hydroxide to introduce a formyl group is called the ______.
questions.explanation: The Reimer–Tiemann reaction introduces a formyl (–CHO) group mainly at the ortho position of phenol.

questions.question: The major product obtained when aqueous KOH reacts with a methyl chloride is generally an ______.
questions.explanation: Aqueous KOH favors nucleophilic substitution, replacing the halogen atom with a hydroxyl group to form an alcohol.

questions.question: The stereochemical process in which a chiral compound is converted into an equimolar mixture of two enantiomers is called ______.
questions.explanation: Racemisation produces equal amounts of both enantiomers, resulting in an optically inactive mixture.

questions.question: The bond between carbon and halogen in haloarenes possesses ______ character due to resonance.
questions.explanation: Resonance between the halogen lone pair and the aromatic ring gives the C–X bond partial double-bond character.

questions.question: Why is phenol more acidic than ethanol?
options: The phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised
Ethanol has a weaker C–O bond
questions.explanation: The conjugate base of phenol (phenoxide ion) is resonance stabilised, making phenol more acidic than ethanol.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing boiling point:  Ethane, Ethanol, Dimethyl ether.
questions.explanation: Alcohols exhibit hydrogen bonding, ethers have dipole–dipole interactions and alkanes possess only weak dispersion forces.

questions.question: Why does dry ether have to be used during the preparation of Grignard reagents?
options: It prevents hydrolysis by moisture and stabilises the Grignard reagent
It converts alkyl halides into alcohols
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents are destroyed by traces of water. Dry ether provides an anhydrous medium and stabilises the reagent.

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains why phenol undergoes bromination without using FeBr₃ catalyst?
options: The –OH group activates the benzene ring by resonance
Bromine water is a stronger electrophile than bromine
questions.explanation: The lone pair on oxygen is donated to the aromatic ring by resonance, increasing electron density and making electrophilic substitution easy.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing acidity: Phenol, Ethanol, Water .
questions.explanation: Phenol is most acidic because its conjugate base is resonance stabilised. Water is more acidic than ethanol.

questions.question: Why is o-nitrophenol steam volatile whereas p-nitrophenol is not?
options: o-Nitrophenol has a lower molecular mass
o-Nitrophenol does not contain a nitro group
questions.explanation: o-Nitrophenol forms intramolecular hydrogen bonding, reducing intermolecular association and making it steam volatile. p-Nitrophenol forms intermolecular hydrogen bonds.

questions.question: Which alcohol resists oxidation by KMnO₄, CrO₃ and PCC under ordinary conditions?
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols do not possess an α-hydrogen and therefore resist oxidation under ordinary conditions.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing reactivity towards the Lucas reagent: Propan-2-ol, tert-Butyl alcohol, Ethanol.
questions.explanation: Lucas reagent reacts fastest with tertiary alcohols because they readily form stable carbocations, followed by secondary and then primary alcohols.

questions.question: Phenol reacts with concentrated nitric acid to produce:
questions.explanation: Concentrated nitric acid converts phenol into 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid).

questions.question: Why is sodium phenoxide more reactive than phenol towards electrophilic aromatic substitution?
options: It contains an additional hydroxyl group
The O⁻ ion donates electron density to the ring more strongly than –OH
It has a weaker C–O bond
It is less soluble in water
questions.explanation: The negatively charged oxygen atom in phenoxide donates electron density to the aromatic ring more effectively than the neutral –OH group, making the ring more reactive towards electrophilic substitution.

questions.question: Why does phenol react with aqueous NaOH whereas ethanol does not?
options: Phenol is sufficiently acidic to form sodium phenoxide
Phenol is a stronger base than ethanol
questions.explanation: Phenol is more acidic because the phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised. Therefore, it reacts with NaOH, unlike ethanol.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing acidity: Phenol, p-Nitrophenol, p-Cresol.
questions.explanation: The electron-withdrawing nitro group increases acidity, whereas the electron-donating methyl group decreases it.

questions.question: Why does phenol give mainly para-bromophenol along with o-bromophenol when brominated in CS₂ or CHCl₃ at low temperature?
options: The ortho position is completely unreactive
The para position experiences less steric hindrance
The bromine attacks only the para position
Phenol behaves as a meta-directing group
questions.explanation: Although both ortho and para positions are activated, the para position is less sterically hindered, making para-bromophenol the major product.

questions.question: Which alcohol is obtained when methylmagnesium bromide reacts with methanal followed by hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: Reaction of methylmagnesium bromide with methanal produces ethanol after acidic hydrolysis. Methanal always gives a primary alcohol with one additional carbon atom.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing solubility in water: Ethanol, Methanol, Propan-1-ol.
questions.explanation: Water solubility decreases as the hydrophobic alkyl chain becomes longer. Hence Methanol > Ethanol > Propan-1-ol.

questions.question: Why does concentrated H₂SO₄ facilitate dehydration of alcohols?
options: It acts as a reducing agent
It protonates the –OH group, converting it into a better leaving group
It supplies oxygen to the alcohol
It converts alcohols directly into aldehydes
questions.explanation: Concentrated H₂SO₄ protonates the hydroxyl group to form water, which is a much better leaving group, thereby promoting elimination.

questions.question: Which product is formed when phenol undergoes the Kolbe reaction followed by acidification?
questions.explanation: In the Kolbe reaction, sodium phenoxide reacts with CO₂ to form sodium salicylate, which on acidification yields salicylic acid.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing ease of dehydration: Propan-2-ol, tert-Butyl alcohol, Ethanol.
questions.explanation: Ease of dehydration follows carbocation stability: tertiary > secondary > primary alcohol.

questions.question: Phenol is converted into salicylaldehyde by treatment with:
questions.explanation: The Reimer–Tiemann reaction uses chloroform and aqueous sodium hydroxide to introduce a formyl group at the ortho position of phenol.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why phenol is ortho/para directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution?
options: The –OH group withdraws electrons by resonance
The –OH group donates electron density to the ortho and para positions through resonance
The –OH group deactivates the benzene ring
Phenol reacts only through the oxygen atom
The inductive effect alone determines substitution
questions.explanation: The lone pair on oxygen is delocalised into the benzene ring by resonance, increasing electron density mainly at the ortho and para positions and directing electrophilic substitution there.

questions.question: Why does phenol undergo electrophilic substitution more readily than benzene?
options: The –OH group withdraws electrons by resonance
The –OH group donates electron density to the ring by resonance
questions.explanation: The lone pair on oxygen is delocalised into the aromatic ring by resonance, increasing its electron density and making phenol more reactive than benzene towards electrophilic substitution.

questions.question: Which reagent is used in the Kolbe reaction for preparing salicylic acid from sodium phenoxide?
questions.explanation: The Kolbe reaction involves reaction of sodium phenoxide with carbon dioxide under pressure followed by acidification to give salicylic acid.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing boiling point: Propan-2-ol, Propan-1-ol, 2-Methylpropan-2-ol.
questions.explanation: Branching reduces surface area and intermolecular attraction. Hence the boiling point decreases with increasing branching.

questions.question: Why does dehydration of alcohols generally give the more substituted alkene as the major product?
options: The less substituted alkene is always more stable
The double bond always forms at carbon-1
Hydrogen is removed only from the α-carbon
Alcohols undergo substitution instead of elimination
questions.explanation: According to Zaitsev's rule, the more substituted (and therefore more stable) alkene is generally formed as the major product.

questions.question: Which alcohol is obtained when ethylmagnesium bromide reacts with ethanal followed by hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: An aldehyde (other than methanal) reacts with a Grignard reagent to produce a secondary alcohol after hydrolysis. Ethanal with ethylmagnesium bromide gives butan-2-ol.

questions.question: Why must Grignard reagents never be prepared in the presence of water?
options: Water converts the reagent into an alkene
Water protonates and destroys the Grignard reagent
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents are highly reactive and are immediately protonated by water to form hydrocarbons, destroying the reagent.

questions.question: Phenol is converted into phenyl acetate during acetylation using:
questions.explanation: Phenol undergoes acetylation with acetic anhydride (or acetyl chloride) to form phenyl acetate.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing boiling point: Methanol, Ethanol, Propan-1-ol.
questions.explanation: All three alcohols exhibit hydrogen bonding. Boiling point increases with molecular mass, giving Propan-1-ol > Ethanol > Methanol.

questions.question: Why does phenol not give the Lucas test under ordinary conditions?
options: The aryl C–O bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance
questions.explanation: The C–O bond in phenol has partial double-bond character because of resonance and therefore does not undergo the substitution required in the Lucas test.

questions.question: Which reagent is preferred for converting alcohols into alkyl chlorides because its by-products escape as gases, giving a pure product?
questions.explanation: Thionyl chloride (SOCl₂) produces gaseous SO₂ and HCl as by-products, leaving relatively pure alkyl chloride.
